question: Describe the basic steps required to insert a new node at the end of a singly linked list. Assume you are given a pointer to the head node.
expectedPoints: Traverse the list starting from the head node until the current node's 'next' pointer is null (i.e., you reach the last node).
Create the new node and set its data/value.
Set the 'next' pointer of the last node to point to the new node.
Set the 'next' pointer of the new node to null to signify it is now the last node.

question: Compare and contrast the process of deleting a node from the beginning of a linked list versus deleting a node from the beginning of an array. Explain the operational differences and their impact on time complexity.
expectedPoints: Deleting from the beginning of a linked list involves updating the head pointer to the second node and freeing the memory of the old head (O(1) time complexity).
Deleting from the beginning of an array requires shifting all subsequent elements one position to the left to fill the gap, which is an O(n) operation.

The key difference is that the array operation affects all remaining elements (costly for large n), while the linked list operation is constant time regardless of size, highlighting a trade-off between memory structure and efficiency for this specific operation.

question: A common operation is to display all elements in a data structure. Outline a detailed algorithm to display the contents of a doubly linked list in reverse order, starting from the tail and traversing backwards to the head. Explain the role of the 'prev' pointers and how the algorithm ensures termination.
expectedPoints: Start by traversing from the head to find the tail node (if a direct tail pointer is not available), or use the provided tail pointer.
Once at the tail node, print its data.
Use the 'prev' pointer of the current node to move to the previous node in the list.

Continue this process—printing the current node's data and then moving to the previous node via the 'prev' pointer—until the 'prev' pointer of the current node becomes null, indicating the head has been reached and printed.

The algorithm terminates correctly because the 'prev' pointer of the head node is null, which breaks the traversal loop after the head is processed, ensuring every node is visited exactly once in reverse order.
